comment.body:
so i was doing some "research" this weekend into jpm exp.. and i came to wonder if jpm exp is actually entered third friday expo "not quarterly on the 31st".  i base this assumption on 2 things. 1. (4700/5565/6165 strikes assume a spot value of ≈ 5,843...  open on 12/20/24 was 5842 ) 2. quarterly expo values were 05,919.74 H5,929.74 L5,868.86 C5,881.62
which a mean would assume long put of 5605 for recent jpm.   also by looking at volume levels of spx on TV it spikes massively for 3rd.friday/quarterly/monthly expo and shows very little volume for the true quarterly expo on the 31st... so expo could very well be on the 31st, my assumption is that they possibly enter into these collars on 3rd friday expo.

comment.body: I have been reflecting on Alma's share of how to look for deviation of spot / vol mechanism in a positive speed environment, and that kept me wondering how that deviation interplays with zero vanna / vanna flip point. As we recall the vanna flip with positive speed convexity which has positive speed profile above and negative below requires relatively low vol to pass through,, if spot down / vol down under a positive speed environment hinting for an underlying customer buying interest, would there be any reference to know if spot will fall below the vanna flip or customer buy interest will effectively stop the price from falling below?

comment.body: something to note from GS: The second thing to note is on the subject of (realized) volatility, where unlike trend signals – that can in theory change daily – once vol has risen those sizable recent moves are in the time-window and there is more of a mathematical speed limit on how fast vol can subside. The result is that for some of the short-medium dated vol-control products, there are some moderately-sized baseline sell flows, that would take somewhat longer to undo and turn to buying because of the preceding sentence and could grow to more selling if vols increased further.
comment.raw_body: something to note from GS: The second thing to note is on the subject of (realized) volatility, where unlike trend signals – that can in theory change daily – once vol has risen those sizable recent moves are in the time-window and there is more of a mathematical speed limit on how fast vol can subside. The result is that for some of the short-medium dated vol-control products, there are some moderately-sized baseline sell flows, that would take somewhat longer to undo and turn to buying because of the preceding sentence and could grow to more selling if vols increased further.

comment.body: this is interesting too: Once short positions are established however, as is the case from short-medium trend following CTA in US markets now for example, an increase in vol can actually lead to some repurchasing from this subset, because the higher vol is dictating or constraining the notional short size, which is playing out at the same time as other systematic players – like vol-control funds or longer-dated CTA – may be selling the same product. All of this is encapsulated as usual in the Chart 1 which will continue to update as markets move, and where shapes for individual markets and cohorts within the systematic space will look different in comprising that total.
